answer:
Comparing Two Rate Limiting Approaches with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Rate limiting controls how many requests a client can make within a time window, protecting APIs from abuse, DDoS attacks, and runaway scripts. The two most common approaches are Fixed Window — a simple counter that resets at fixed intervals (e.g., 100 requests per minute, resetting at :00) — and Sliding Window Log — which tracks each request's timestamp and counts requests within a rolling window. Each has distinct trade-offs around burst tolerance, memory usage, and implementation complexity.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, rate limiting was essential for two endpoints: POST /api/leave/apply (to prevent duplicate submissions from double-clicks) and POST /api/auth/login (to prevent brute-force password attacks). Manoj initially used Fixed Window via @fastify/rate-limit — 5 login attempts per 15-minute window. However, the sales team discovered that an attacker could make 5 attempts at 14:59 and 5 more at 15:00 (window boundary), effectively getting 10 attempts in 1 minute.

For the login endpoint, Manoj switched to Sliding Window using a Redis sorted set that tracked each attempt's timestamp. For the leave-apply endpoint, Fixed Window was kept because the "burst at boundary" issue was acceptable — applying for leave twice was caught by the backend's idempotency check anyway.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// Fixed Window — using @fastify/rate-limit (leave-apply endpoint)
import rateLimit from '@fastify/rate-limit';

app.register(rateLimit, {
  global: false, // don't apply globally
});

app.post('/api/leave/apply', {
  config: {
    rateLimit: {
      max: 3,        // 3 submissions
      timeWindow: 60000, // per minute
      keyGenerator: (req) => req.headers['x-user-id'],
    },
  },
}, leaveApplyHandler);

// Sliding Window Log — custom Redis implementation (login endpoint)
import Redis from 'ioredis';
const redis = new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL!);

async function slidingWindowRateLimit(
  key: string,
  maxRequests: number,
  windowMs: number
): Promise<{ allowed: boolean; remaining: number; retryAfter?: number }> {
  const now = Date.now();
  const windowStart = now - windowMs;

  // Atomic pipeline: remove old entries, add current, count
  const pipeline = redis.pipeline();
  pipeline.zremrangebyscore(key, 0, windowStart);   // prune expired
  pipeline.zadd(key, now.toString(), `${now}-${Math.random()}`); // add request
  pipeline.zcard(key);                              // count in window
  pipeline.expire(key, Math.ceil(windowMs / 1000)); // auto-cleanup

  const results = await pipeline.exec();
  const count = results![2][1] as number;

  if (count > maxRequests) {
    // Find oldest entry to calculate retry-after
    const oldest = await redis.zrange(key, 0, 0, 'WITHSCORES');
    const retryAfter = oldest.length >= 2
      ? windowMs - (now - parseInt(oldest[1]))
      : windowMs;
    return { allowed: false, remaining: 0, retryAfter };
  }

  return { allowed: true, remaining: maxRequests - count };
}

// Usage in login route
app.post('/api/auth/login', async (req, reply) => {
  const ip = req.ip;
  const result = await slidingWindowRateLimit(
    `login:${ip}`, 5, 15 * 60 * 1000 // 5 attempts per 15 min
  );

  if (!result.allowed) {
    reply.code(429)
      .header('Retry-After', Math.ceil(result.retryAfter! / 1000))
      .send({ error: 'Too many login attempts' });
    return;
  }

  reply.header('X-RateLimit-Remaining', result.remaining);
  return loginHandler(req, reply);
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After deploying the sliding window rate limiter for login, Manoj received reports that legitimate users were getting rate-limited even after a successful login. The issue: the rate limit key was based on req.ip, and the office had 30 employees behind a single NAT IP. All 30 shared the same 5-attempt limit.

Manoj fixed it by using a compound key: login:${ip}:${email} — rate limiting per IP+email combination. He also added a separate, higher-limit per-IP counter (50 attempts per 15 min across all emails) to still protect against distributed brute-force attacks from a single IP. The fix was tested by simulating 30 concurrent logins from the same IP with different emails — all succeeded.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Rate limiting by IP blocks shared office/VPN usersUse compound keys (IP + user identifier); implement higher per-IP limits for known corporate IPs
Sliding window sorted sets grow unbounded for abusive clientsSet Redis EXPIRE on the key equal to the window duration; prune old entries in the same pipeline as the check
Rate limit headers missing from error responsesAdd X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and Retry-After headers to both success and 429 responses

7. Validation Strategy

  • Boundary test: Make exactly maxRequests calls — all should succeed; the next call should get 429
  • Window expiry test: Make maxRequests calls, wait for window to expire, make another — should succeed
  • Concurrency test: Use Promise.all to send 10 simultaneous requests — verify exactly maxRequests succeed and the rest get 429
  • Retry-After accuracy: Verify the Retry-After header value is within 1 second of the actual time until the window opens

Designing a Practical Rate Limiting Implementation

1. Definition & Context

A practical rate limiting implementation for a mid-size product must handle multiple tiers (anonymous, authenticated, admin), multiple dimensions (per-user, per-IP, per-endpoint), and gracefully degrade under load. The implementation should be centralized (not scattered across individual routes) and observable (dashboards showing who's being rate-limited and why).

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In Manoj's ContactsHub project, the POST /api/contacts/bulk-upload endpoint accepted CSV files with up to 500 contacts per batch. Without rate limiting, a user could accidentally trigger 10 uploads in rapid succession (e.g., by refreshing the page after a slow upload), creating 5,000 database writes in seconds. Manoj implemented a tiered rate limiting strategy:

  • Tier 1 — Global: 200 requests/minute across all endpoints (protects against scripted abuse)
  • Tier 2 — Endpoint-specific: Bulk upload limited to 2 requests per 5 minutes per user
  • Tier 3 — Cost-based: Each bulk upload "costs" 50 rate limit tokens (vs 1 for a regular GET), so a user who uploads uses most of their global budget

3. Architecture Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// rate-limiter.ts — Tiered rate limiting middleware for Fastify
import Redis from 'ioredis';
import { FastifyRequest, FastifyReply } from 'fastify';

const redis = new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL!);

interface RateLimitConfig {
  max: number;
  windowSec: number;
  cost?: number; // tokens consumed per request
  keyPrefix: string;
}

async function checkLimit(
  key: string, max: number, windowSec: number, cost: number
): Promise<{ allowed: boolean; remaining: number }> {
  const current = await redis.incrby(key, cost);
  if (current === cost) {
    // First request — set expiry
    await redis.expire(key, windowSec);
  }
  return {
    allowed: current <= max,
    remaining: Math.max(0, max - current),
  };
}

// Factory for rate limit preHandlers
export function rateLimit(config: RateLimitConfig) {
  return async (req: FastifyRequest, reply: FastifyReply) => {
    const userId = req.headers['x-user-id'] || req.ip;
    const key = `rl:${config.keyPrefix}:${userId}`;
    const cost = config.cost || 1;

    const result = await checkLimit(
      key, config.max, config.windowSec, cost
    );

    reply.header('X-RateLimit-Limit', config.max);
    reply.header('X-RateLimit-Remaining', result.remaining);

    if (!result.allowed) {
      const ttl = await redis.ttl(key);
      reply.header('Retry-After', ttl);
      reply.code(429).send({
        error: 'Rate limit exceeded',
        retryAfter: ttl,
        tier: config.keyPrefix,
      });
    }
  };
}

// Usage in ContactsHub routes
app.addHook('preHandler', rateLimit({
  keyPrefix: 'global',
  max: 200,
  windowSec: 60,
}));

app.post('/api/contacts/bulk-upload', {
  preHandler: [
    rateLimit({ keyPrefix: 'bulk', max: 2, windowSec: 300 }),
    rateLimit({ keyPrefix: 'global', max: 200, windowSec: 60, cost: 50 }),
  ],
}, bulkUploadHandler);

app.get('/api/contacts', {
  preHandler: rateLimit({
    keyPrefix: 'contacts-read',
    max: 60,
    windowSec: 60,
  }),
}, contactListHandler);

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After deploying tiered rate limiting in ContactsHub, Manoj discovered a race condition: the INCRBY and EXPIRE commands were not atomic. Under high concurrency, two simultaneous first-requests could both execute INCRBY (getting values 1 and 2), but only one would set EXPIRE. If the one that set EXPIRE was the second request, the key had no expiry race. Worse, if the EXPIRE command failed (Redis hiccup), the key persisted forever, permanently blocking the user.

Fix: Replaced the two-command sequence with a Lua script that atomically increments and sets expiry in a single EVAL call. This guaranteed that INCRBY and EXPIRE always executed together, eliminating the race condition.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
INCRBY + EXPIRE race condition on first requestUse a Lua script or Redis SET NX EX pattern to atomically set the counter with expiry
Cost-based limiting confuses users ("why am I blocked after 4 GETs?")Return X-RateLimit-Cost header showing how many tokens each request consumed; document in API docs
Redis outage means all requests are either blocked or unlimitedFail open with an in-memory fallback counter; log a warning so ops team knows rate limiting is degraded

7. Validation Strategy

  • Tier isolation test: Exhaust endpoint-specific limit, verify global limit still has budget for other endpoints
  • Cost accuracy test: Make one bulk upload (cost=50), verify global remaining shows 150 (not 199)
  • Concurrency test: 20 parallel requests from same user — verify exactly max succeed, rest get 429
  • TTL test: Exhaust limit, check Retry-After header, wait that duration, retry — should succeed

Comparing Two Event-Driven Design Approaches with Trade-offs

1. Definition & Context

Event-Driven Design decouples components by having them communicate through events rather than direct calls. The two primary approaches are Event Emitter (in-process) — using Node.js EventEmitter or RxJS Subjects for components within the same process — and Message Broker (inter-process) — using Redis Pub/Sub, RabbitMQ, or Kafka for communication between separate services. The choice depends on whether components are co-located in the same process or distributed across services.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, event-driven design was needed for the leave approval workflow. When a manager approved a leave request, multiple things had to happen: (1) update the leave status in PostgreSQL, (2) decrement the employee's leave balance, (3) send a Socket.io real-time notification, (4) send an email notification, and (5) update the team calendar.

In-process EventEmitter was used within the Fastify monolith because all services ran in the same Node.js process. The approval handler emitted leave.approved, and separate listener modules handled each side effect independently.

However, when Manoj later needed to add a Slack notification (a separate Node.js bot service), he introduced Redis Pub/Sub for cross-service communication. The main Fastify app published leave.approved to a Redis channel, and the Slack bot subscribed to it.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// In-Process EventEmitter — HR Leave System
import { EventEmitter } from 'events';

const leaveEvents = new EventEmitter();

// Approval handler emits event
async function approveLeave(leaveId: string, managerId: string) {
  const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.update({
    where: { id: leaveId },
    data: { status: 'approved', approvedBy: managerId },
    include: { employee: true },
  });

  // Fire and forget — listeners handle side effects
  leaveEvents.emit('leave.approved', {
    leaveId: leave.id,
    employeeId: leave.employeeId,
    employeeName: leave.employee.name,
    days: leave.days,
    startDate: leave.startDate,
  });

  return leave;
}

// Listener: Update balance
leaveEvents.on('leave.approved', async (data) => {
  await prisma.leaveBalance.update({
    where: { employeeId: data.employeeId },
    data: { remaining: { decrement: data.days } },
  });
});

// Listener: Real-time notification via Socket.io
leaveEvents.on('leave.approved', (data) => {
  io.to(`user:${data.employeeId}`).emit('notification', {
    type: 'leave-approved',
    message: `Your ${data.days}-day leave starting ${data.startDate} was approved`,
  });
});

// Redis Pub/Sub — Cross-service (Slack bot)
import Redis from 'ioredis';
const pub = new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL!);

// In Fastify app — publish after approval
leaveEvents.on('leave.approved', async (data) => {
  await pub.publish('leave.approved', JSON.stringify(data));
});

// In Slack bot service (separate process)
const sub = new Redis(process.env.REDIS_URL!);
sub.subscribe('leave.approved');
sub.on('message', async (channel, message) => {
  const data = JSON.parse(message);
  await slackClient.chat.postMessage({
    channel: '#hr-leaves',
    text: `✅ Leave approved: ${data.employeeName} — ${data.days} days from ${data.startDate}`,
  });
});

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj encountered a critical bug where leave balance was sometimes not decremented after approval. The approval itself worked (status changed to 'approved'), but the balance remained unchanged. The issue: the leave.approved EventEmitter listener for balance updates threw an unhandled PrismaClientKnownRequestError when two approvals for the same employee happened within milliseconds (concurrent balance decrement on the same row).

Since EventEmitter swallows errors in listeners by default (unless an error event listener is registered), the error was silently lost. Manoj fixed it by: (1) adding leaveEvents.on('error', (err) => logger.error(err)), (2) wrapping the balance update in a retry with prisma.$transaction for serializable isolation, and (3) adding a reconciliation job that compared approved leaves against balance deductions every hour.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
EventEmitter swallows errors in listeners silentlyAlways register a global error handler: emitter.on('error', handler); wrap each listener in try-catch with structured logging
Redis Pub/Sub loses messages when subscriber is downUse Redis Streams instead of Pub/Sub for durable messaging; or implement a "catch-up" query on subscriber startup
Event handlers creating circular dependencies (A emits → B emits → A emits)Enforce unidirectional event flow; add a sourceEvent field to prevent re-processing

7. Validation Strategy

  • Side-effect test: Approve a leave in test, verify all 4 side effects executed (balance, socket, email, Slack) by mocking each listener
  • Error isolation test: Make one listener throw — verify other listeners still execute and the error is logged
  • Redis Pub/Sub test: Publish event, verify Slack bot subscriber receives it within 100ms
  • Reconciliation test: Simulate a dropped balance update, run reconciliation job, verify it detects and fixes the discrepancy

Designing a Practical Event-Driven Implementation

1. Definition & Context

A practical event-driven implementation for a mid-size product uses events to decouple command execution from side effects. The core action (e.g., approving a leave) completes synchronously and returns to the user, while downstream effects (notifications, analytics, cache invalidation) happen asynchronously via event listeners. This keeps the critical path fast and allows adding new behaviors without modifying the core logic.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the leave request lifecycle had 6 state transitions: draft → submitted → pending_l1 → pending_l2 → pending_l3 → approved/rejected. Each transition triggered different side effects — submitted triggered a notification to the L1 manager, pending_l2 triggered an email to L2, approved triggered balance update + calendar sync + Slack notification. Rather than writing giant if/else blocks in each route handler, Manoj designed a state machine with event hooks.

3. Event Flow Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// leave-state-machine.ts — Event-driven state machine
import { EventEmitter } from 'events';
import { prisma } from './db';
import { io } from './socket';

type LeaveStatus = 'draft' | 'submitted' | 'pending_l1'
  | 'pending_l2' | 'pending_l3' | 'approved' | 'rejected';

const validTransitions: Record = {
  draft: ['submitted'],
  submitted: ['pending_l1'],
  pending_l1: ['pending_l2', 'rejected'],
  pending_l2: ['pending_l3', 'rejected'],
  pending_l3: ['approved', 'rejected'],
  approved: [],
  rejected: [],
};

class LeaveStateMachine extends EventEmitter {
  async transition(leaveId: string, to: LeaveStatus, actorId: string) {
    const leave = await prisma.leaveRequest.findUniqueOrThrow({
      where: { id: leaveId },
      include: { employee: true },
    });

    const from = leave.status as LeaveStatus;
    if (!validTransitions[from]?.includes(to)) {
      throw new Error(`Invalid transition: ${from} → ${to}`);
    }

    // Update state in DB (source of truth)
    const updated = await prisma.leaveRequest.update({
      where: { id: leaveId },
      data: { status: to, lastActorId: actorId },
      include: { employee: true },
    });

    // Emit typed event for this specific transition
    this.emit(`${from}→${to}`, updated);
    // Also emit generic state-change event
    this.emit('transition', { from, to, leave: updated });

    return updated;
  }
}

const machine = new LeaveStateMachine();

// Register listeners for each transition
machine.on('submitted→pending_l1', async (leave) => {
  // Notify L1 manager via Socket.io
  const l1Manager = await prisma.approvalChain.findFirst({
    where: { employeeId: leave.employeeId, level: 1 },
  });
  if (l1Manager) {
    io.to(`user:${l1Manager.managerId}`).emit('notification', {
      type: 'new-leave-request',
      message: `${leave.employee.name} requested ${leave.days} days leave`,
      leaveId: leave.id,
    });
  }
});

machine.on('pending_l3→approved', async (leave) => {
  // Side effect 1: Update balance
  await prisma.leaveBalance.update({
    where: { employeeId: leave.employeeId },
    data: { remaining: { decrement: leave.days } },
  });

  // Side effect 2: Notify employee
  io.to(`user:${leave.employeeId}`).emit('notification', {
    type: 'leave-approved',
    message: `Your leave from ${leave.startDate} was approved!`,
  });
});

// Log all transitions for audit
machine.on('transition', async ({ from, to, leave }) => {
  await prisma.auditLog.create({
    data: {
      entity: 'leave',
      entityId: leave.id,
      action: `${from}→${to}`,
      actorId: leave.lastActorId,
    },
  });
});

export { machine };

5. Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Manoj encountered a bug where the L1 manager received duplicate Socket.io notifications for the same leave request. Investigation revealed the state machine was being instantiated in a module that was imported by two different Fastify plugins, creating two separate LeaveStateMachine instances — each registering its own listeners. When a transition occurred, both instances' listeners fired.

Diagnosis: Added a console.log('listener count:', machine.listenerCount('submitted→pending_l1')) — it showed 2. Fix: Made the state machine a singleton by exporting the instance (not the class) and using a module-level variable. Also added a startup check: if (machine.listenerCount(event) > 1) throw new Error('Duplicate listener') as a safeguard.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Multiple module imports creating duplicate event listenersExport singleton instance; add listener-count assertion at startup; use once() for one-time setup events
State machine allows invalid transitions silentlyThrow on invalid transitions; log the attempted transition for debugging; add TypeScript exhaustive checks on state
Event listeners failing silently prevent critical side effectsWrap each listener in try-catch; emit a listener.error event; add reconciliation jobs for critical side effects (balance updates)

7. Validation Strategy

  • State machine test: Test all valid transitions succeed and all invalid transitions throw
  • Listener isolation test: Mock each listener, trigger transition, verify only the correct listeners fire
  • Duplicate prevention test: Import state machine from two modules, verify listener count is 1 (singleton works)
  • Audit trail test: Run a full leave lifecycle (draft → approved), verify audit log has all 5 transition entries
